video_title: Lawfare Daily: Taking Stock of the Ukraine-Russia Talks
transcript: [music] >> We could debate where where the fault lies for this, but I don't think there is really a credible alternative that Putin's looking at for even to explore a process that could be there to explore what a credible alternative to continued fighting might look like. >> It's Lawfare Podcast. My name is Mykhailo Soldatenko, Lawfare contributing editor, and I'm joined today by Eric Ciaramella, senior fellow at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, and a contributing editor at Lawfare, and Samuel Charap, >> [music] >> distinguished chair in Russia and Eurasia policy and senior political scientist at RAND Corporation. >> The sense I got is that they've moved away from some of these stranger kind of hybrid proposals on the Donbas, and the territorial issue is just sort of frozen for the time being while, you know, Ukraine is fighting for more of an advantage strategically. >> Today we are taking stock of the US-led negotiations between Ukraine and Russia. After active diplomatic engagement in >> [music] >> 2025 and early 2026, negotiations became virtually dormant. >> [music] >> We are discussing the main issues that parties addressed during the active phase of the talks, [music] and pondering about potential solutions for future negotiations. So, Eric, Sam, last time we talked, both of you agreed that one of the main obstacles to a negotiated settlement is Russia's war optimism. Basically, their conviction that they can achieve their objectives on the battlefield, and that time works in their favor. Given recent Ukrainian drone strikes, including deep into Russia, that disrupts Russian logistics, create fuel shortages, and bring war closer to ordinary Russians, do you think there is a chance to decrease Russian war optimism in such a way and make Kremlin to make reasonable concession, or you think this will just lead to further escalation and the continuation of the war of attrition? Let's start with with Eric. >> Great. You know, we're definitely in the middle of a different phase of this war whereby Ukraine, through its drone strikes, not only deep inside Russia, but also the so-called middle strikes against Russia's logistics corridor that connects Crimea to Russia uh has put a significant amount of pressure on the Russian military effort in a way that we haven't seen so far in this war. So, I think the the Russian military and, you know, more broadly, the Russian economic sort of managers have been forced to contemplate and make trade-offs that they did not have to in earlier years in this war. That being said, I would hesitate to say that we're in the midst of seeing Russia's war optimism really diminish because, ultimately, what it comes down to is one man's calculation. And I have yet to see real evidence that the challenges that Russia's facing have been interpreted by Putin himself as fundamentally necessitating a change in in strategic approach. So, it could very well be that, you know, what we're seeing from the outside is serious enough in terms of the challenges inside Russia that Putin simply hasn't grappled with them yet because, you know, whether he's isolated, getting fed bad information from his commanders, kind of ignoring the problems or so on. You know, but I think we won't really the the challenge with kind of looking into the crystal ball here is that because this war hinges so much on one person's decision-making and he himself is a black box and he may very well not have any idea himself what course he wants to take over the next 6 to 12 months. We won't really see a change in Russian approach until the decision has been made at the top. So, all of these kind of uh signals that we're trying to interpret, reading the tea leaves, there was this recent essay by a Russian oligarch in The Economist that people are making a lot of you know, hay out of. To me, it's all it's not necessarily background noise. I mean, it is kind of important to to analyze these different signals, but to me it doesn't it's not yet determinative of what the the Russian leadership and specifically Putin is thinking. So, you know, that being said, I think the course that Ukraine is is taking makes a lot of sense and I think if we were to assess where Ukraine stands strategically now versus a year ago, you know, whereas the Russian side of the equation is a black box as I said, the Western side appears to have changed dramatically where the narrative, you know, when the Trump administration came into office last year was that Ukraine was on an inexorable path to defeat. I think now, and you saw it just at the NATO summit recently, there's a sense that actually, you know, the the initiative at least on the battlefield is trending somewhat in Ukraine's favor and that, you know, ultimately, maybe the jury's still out on how this war ends and I think it's given a little bit of a push to Ukraine's efforts to try and potentially secure a little bit longer runway of of Western and specifically US support. >> Eric, just a short follow-up. So, during one of the press conferences, Putin acknowledged uh that there are problems, even though he minimized the impact of those in his speech. Do you think that he might he may understand the scope of problems, but just ready to incur a costs because considering how much he has invested in this war. Do you think that's also an option? >> I do. And I mean, it's clear that he's been willing to uh sustain a much higher level of pain than Western policy makers, I think, expected. You know, if we would have cast forward and seen the degree of, you know, economic pressure that Russia's been under, he's he's certainly been willing to subject the Russian population to it. You know, whether whether he truly understands and again, whether we truly understand. I mean, we're looking at this from the outside and we have just fragmentary information and because, you know, it's so hard for Americans and Europeans to really understand what's going on on the ground in Russia these days, you know, we may we may be overstating it, we may be understating it. So, I you know, for sure he recognizes that there are problems, but I do think going back to what I was saying earlier that he doesn't at least there's no evidence at this point that he sees those problems as severe enough or unmanageable enough that he needs to change course, strategically speaking. >> Uh Sam, what are your thoughts on that? And do you think Ukraine right now is in better position than it was uh when negotiations virtually became dormant in early 2026? >> Well, I think, you know, without question that the the factors that Eric identified are objectively, if you were to objectively assess the Russia's military prospects, they're, you know, nowhere near as bright, so to speak, as they were in February 2026. But, as he also notes, Eric, the key question is how those objective factors get translated into the subjective perceptions, and particularly those of one man. And then, there's the added question about, like, let's just say he might even know all of this, and his optimism might be diminished. He's very little incentive to let that on in public, right? And so, the public bravado that we hear from Putin a lot doesn't even necessarily tell us what's going on in in his head, given that he is speaking to a domestic audience. He's got, you know, legislative elections coming up, and he has his own domestic popularity, which is reportedly sinking, to to worry about. So, I think the best we can say is that, based on observable empirical evidence, that that Russia should be less optimistic, and therefore, Ukraine is in a relatively better position. However, question is how you convert pressure into the kind of pressure that Ukraine is as is putting on Russia into a better outcome for Ukraine. And there, you know, if we've seen this historically, if, you know, one side lacks a credible alternative to continuing the war, they're more likely to continue it, even if, you know, the the optimism factor isn't there. And I think that connects to the question about negotiations, because at this point, and, you know, we could debate where where the fault lies for this, but I don't think there is really a credible alternative that Putin is looking at for even to explore a process that could be there to explore what a credible alternative to continued fighting might look like. So, you know, I think objectively, the situation has improved for Ukraine in this context, you know, relatively speaking, but how that can be converted into a better outcome in the context of a endgame scenario is a is sort of a separate, you know, one level a separate question in a way. >> So, the negotiations were structured where Washington would actually act as a mediator, even though it supported one of the parties to the war for a long time. And Europeans were out of negotiations, but they were coordinating the position with Ukrainians. Do you think this structure can produce a negotiated settlement, or it should be changed? And what would be your advice about how to structure negotiations, Eric? >> Can it work? I think theoretically, yes, if you had American negotiators who really understood the issues deeply and were credible interlocutors with the Europeans, given the broader state of affairs in transatlantic relations, I think there's very obvious uh skepticism on the European part that the American side is taking into account European interests in these talks. Uh I think you're right that the US side, I mean, it's really inconceivable for the United States to be a true mediator in this sense. And I don't think a mediator in a classical sense is really even needed in this case. But, I think what's been disappointing is that there have been several windows of opportunity since this administration took office to potentially get a more serious negotiation underway. The Ukrainian side uh while reluctant at first came around to seeing it as valuable. I think the Ukrainian side has tried to you know, use these talks in a way to actually make meaningful progress. And I think it's the the American side's lack of organization, expertise, uh policy coordination. I mean, the sheer fact that you have two American negotiators without any team of their own who are simultaneously also responsible for negotiations with Iran. I mean, to me it it it's really makes very little sense and I think it's unfortunate because the administration has repeatedly squandered opportunities to get something more serious going. So, in a theoretical sense, yes, a trilateral format with significant coordination with Europeans and input from them could work. I think with the current cast of characters, um I'm pretty skeptical if they return to that same model that they've used in the past. >> Sam, your thoughts? >> So, I want to start with the question about mediator because I've actually I found this to be um something that, you know, you hear actually from including European leaders like Macron after the G7 or no, it was actually the European Council meeting which came immediately after the G7 where he said that, you know, Europe supports Ukraine and therefore it can't be a mediator. I think if you look historically at how conflict negotiations have worked, the mediator isn't necessarily neutral. Um just look at the US role in negotiating, you know, the end of the Yom Kippur War and uh subsequent agreements between Egypt and Israel and Israel and Syria respectively. It's not like there was any question about where US loyalties were at that point. I mean, at the beginning of that war, the US didn't even have diplomatic relations with Egypt. Clearly, you know, uh the US sympathies were were on one side of this and the US was in fact militarily supporting Israel. So, the fact that the US continues to both sell weapons to the Europeans for Ukraine and provide intelligence and various other support, even if that much lower levels than before, does not disqualify it from being an effective mediator. And I think we need to put aside the idea that a mediator has to be neutral. Um I think that's been a hold-up for the Europeans taking a more active role in this. So, that's one piece. So, in theory, and here I agree with Eric, like you could have US mediation. It's more about the parties' view about the mediators' objectives. If the parties think that the mediator is there to, you know, find a uh uh a a good deal um for them both or to find some sort of fair compromise, then that can work. I do think that you know, a more direct involvement of the Europeans would be quite useful um both because of the deficiencies in the US approach that Eric highlighted uh and because they, you know, they have stakes in the outcome. They care more about the details than the US does. And so, you know, I I think having them involved in some way would be useful for a better outcome. There's a problem, of course, with representation and you know, you're not going to you can't have 30 representatives for Europe around the table with uh at that point. And they they have yet to settle on or appoint a point person for this. That's the mediation question. I think really that the problem with the US approach so far has been less about the fact that the US has been leading it and more in the deficiencies on process. I mean, we just we haven't seen things that are associated with how this has been done successfully in the past. So, you know, a continuous process where we've seen in this case an episodic one. So, you know, historically when you dealing with complicated international disputes like this, you need to actually get people in a room like regularly, not every few months, or in this case, you know, we're we're almost going on six. That just doesn't work to get through these issues. You know, you need to have some idea of getting all the issues on the table at the same time so that there's a degree of trade space between them, and we can get to this later. Like that hasn't happened. You want to maintain a degree of confidentiality when so far these have all been media circuses really around these things. You know, you want to actually have the parties be the ones who are owning the process and the outcomes. And here again, the US has sort of both ran with its own ideas about how the process should be run, and proposed its own solutions without seemingly much input from at least both sides, uh as opposed to letting them sort of take the baton and thus be bought into it, uh and have some stake in its success. So, I could keep going. I mean, I think that the process matters here, and it's it's treated as a secondary thing. Like all you need to do is get the people in the room. But actually, it kind of matters about like how you structure it. And I think we've seen that it's going to take a whole lot more than uh an episodic you know, meeting every once in a while to work through a lot of these complicated issues. >> Yes, um what do you think? What's the theory of the case from Vitkov and Kushner? Why they structured it in such a way? What would be their argument? Do you think maybe they think that experts would complicate, prolong the process? What do you think? What's the theory of the case there? >> I mean, I don't want to put words in their mouth, but I think that the based on what I've seen Kushner's comments on his experience in the first term negotiating the Abraham Accords is that he doesn't have much um regard for the traditional approach to conflict diplomacy um and and he thinks that taking a more freewheeling business-like based on his business experience approach is sort of how you get things done and he's was reinforced in that by his perceived success with the Abraham Accords and even early on with Gaza. That, you know, this wasn't done the traditional way and they're trying to do these things with creating new structures and avoiding the UN and not having any people in their teams who are you know, who do this for a living. Yeah, I think it's a sort of view that the traditional way of doing things has failed and that they're going to try something based on how they know negotiation. >> So, turning to substantive questions. So, based on prior negotiations, it looks like the territorial questions was not were was not the most important for the Russians. But right now it appears and partly represented it as so this is the core thing. And then Zelenskyy said and you US representative noted that this is the only issue that remained to be resolved. Like 10% of the deal. So, do you have any explanation why the territorial question became central as opposed to other questions? Sam? >> Well, I mean, I think there are a few reasons. One is that the US made it central. That it was put on the table by Steve Witcoff in the the meeting in Moscow before Anchorage. And you know, I think it was Therefore, it became the first order issue when it might not have been had there been a different approach. And then I think, you know, the Russians came away from Anchorage with the view that this was already decided and essentially the the Donbas was like gifted to them. And that's what the US promised, at least this is what is alleged. And so there's a second element here, which is like, okay, we got a concession. Why should we give it up if it's already been provided to us? Of course, this this doesn't really make much sense given that the US can't like snap its fingers and make Ukraine withdraw, I think as it has discovered over the last year. And I think the second piece is maybe more problematic, which is that it has become, I think, it will be challenging for Putin to end the fighting without having taken the rest of the Donbas, given the number of times he has publicly committed to that. And I think territory has grown in importance as a result of the repeated, you know, invocation of it by Putin, which I think he himself has boxed himself into a corner. And you know, this began all the way back in the fall of '22 when he announced the annexation, therefore giving himself less room for maneuver. I think we've discovered that there's a bit more room for maneuver and that no one's talking about all of Zaporizhzhia and Kherson anymore. But and I do think this is not necessarily a insurmountable problem, but it is to a certain extent a problem of his own creation as well as the sort of US negotiation one could argue mistake. >> Eric, what are your thoughts on that? And do you think it's possible to reach a ceasefire along the line of contact if we will include the negotiations of other issues and connect them with the territorial question? >> Yeah, I I basically agree with uh Sam's assessment that it was a a big American blunder to put this on the table early on and to reduce the negotiation to, you know, land swaps, which Steve Whitcoff talked about, you know, repeatedly as if it were as simple as just redrawing lines on a map. Even if it were that simple, that in and of itself is highly complicated, but I think the Russians played a part in creating this problem insofar as I think they leaned in to Whitcoff's naive approach and led him to believe that the territory was the main issue and if only the American side could unlock this door by pressuring the Ukrainians to withdraw, everything else would fall into place. And my view is that were this door to be unlocked by dint of this pressure on the Ukrainian side, which uh again didn't pan out, it would have opened into another anteroom with a bunch of other locked doors on much more complicated issues related to Ukraine's military and broader European security and yada yada yada, all these other things that have been on Russia's laundry list. So, I I think it was a combination of Whitcoff's blunder and Russian craftiness uh about trying to get an early major concession and then pocket it and move the goalposts that we are in this place. I'm not sure on Sam's point that the the territory has become sort of a thing in itself and it's now very hard for Putin to retreat from. You know, I I don't know. I think he's still firmly enough in control of the Russian system whereby if he redefined victory uh, he can kind of talk his way out of anything. That gets to your point, Mykhailo, on a ceasefire in place along the current line. >> If I may, just to follow up on that, do you think it's still, even though he controls the media environment in Russia and everything, there are still limits considering how many Russians died? You still need to come up with some sort of a tangible outcome in order to present at home. And so, if you don't have demilitarization, if you don't have Ukraine uh, neutrality, so that maybe the territory sounds like a something that ordinary people can understand. Like returning Donbas that Putin said was sort of his goal back from 2022. >> Listen, I think the Russians will take whatever they can get and they will spin it as a victory. I think there are many ways to construct the disaster of this war as a victory. As you saw with some of the recent articles and Telegram posts by people in the so-called patriotic camp where they've been trying to make the case that, okay, Russia's initial aims in the so-called special military operation were achieved and then more and, you know, resetting and taking a pause, maybe a long-term pause, an armistice, actually is in Russia's favor. So I see the possibility of redefining victory as available to Putin at any point that he doesn't necessarily need something more on the quote-unquote demilitarization side or neutrality or anything like that. Of course, he would love to have those. But, that's for the question of selling it to the public. The bigger question is what he feels he needs and wants for his own sense of accomplishment and winning. And I think, I mean, it's clear because the war hasn't stopped that Putin doesn't feel like it's there yet. So, it could be that this pressure that we were talking about at the beginning of the podcast gets him into a place where he does end up scaling back his objectives. It just we we're not there yet. So, we don't know until the moment when he decides that it's not working. And it was similar with early in the war when Putin made the decision to pull back troops from Kyiv, you know, he was very bullish. I mean, as as Sam was saying, you know, there's a lot of bravado in public up until the moment that they make a pivot, and then they make you think that the pivot was the plan all along. And in that case, it was a goodwill gesture, and of course we did that, and you know, yada yada yada. So, you won't know it until you see it, but you could be running at 100 miles an hour uh on the Russian side saying, you know, victory or nothing, and then suddenly they pivot on a dime, and Putin is selling a different vision. So, I think from the from the domestic narrative standpoint, Russians would be most Russians, certainly not the hardcore, you know, ultra-nationalist types, but the vast majority of Russians would be fine with whatever theory of victory they're sold in propaganda channels. >> Sam, what do you think about this and what are the issues that can be used to sort of decrease the importance of the territorial question? >> So, I I think this is sort of a negotiating tactic question. You know, given that territory is a zero-sum issue, if I were constructing this negotiation, I would put that to the side and try to work on everything else and then come back to it and and basically test the proposition that you put forward, Mykhailo, that uh under the right circumstances, if some of the other political and security issues were addressed, that this issue might fade in importance. I don't know that's to be the case, but none of us do because, you know, we haven't played out that uh scenario and I think that is the that would be the move in terms of what where a mediator would would should take the conversation going forward, just to say, "Okay, we're not going to get agreement up front on this territory issue. Let's work on other issues and then come back to it uh at some later point." And, you know, maybe it will become easier to deal with if there are mutual understandings on some of the other big big picture issues. Uh I don't know, but I think that testing that proposition is absolutely critical in terms of next steps in in a in a in a negotiation process. >> Eric, what do you think about proposals that were put forward about territorial questions, about uh mutual withdrawal of Russian and Ukrainian forces, so-called Donyland, so the creating a special economic zone on the territory that Ukraine currently controls? Do you think this is still relevant or with the change of the trends of the Ukrainian negotiation position, it's right now can be irrelevant? >> I mean, I think some of those proposals uh were a bit too creative. It's good It's good to be creative, but there's a a limit to that. I mean, at some point the the mechanisms for some kind of demilitarized zone and and sort of monitoring and verification along the front would be necessary. I mean, that's sort of a basic requirement of any ceasefire holding. And so, how you structure a withdrawal and so on, you know, would have to be the the subject of negotiation. My understanding is that actually on the more technical sides, if you take the the question of the Donbas and its ultimate disposition off the table, that some of the talks that happened in the Middle East earlier this year, where you had the head of Russian military intelligence participating, that there was actually a reasonably constructive [clears throat] fact-based discussion of how a ceasefire could be implemented and and some sort of, you know, pullback or or whatnot. But, that would be only if the line were agreed on the political level. And then, how would you implement it and so on? I mean, that there's a lot of complications here related to, you know, third-country forces and different kinds of verification measures. Sam has written a lot about this, you know, the ability to use remote sensing and so on and so forth. These to me are all problems that can be worked out if there's the political will on both sides and the general acceptance that, you know, we have a line and we're working from a common sheet of music here. But, I think some of the other like stranger proposals that it would become a completely demilitarized zone, maybe policed by Elon Musk's drone sort of army, to me it sounds kind of dystopian. And I I just don't see how those proposals go beyond concepts that sort of appear fine in in a single paragraph on a paper, but how you can actually implement those in reality. So, the sense I got is that they've moved away from some of these stranger kind of hybrid proposals on the Donbas, and the territorial issue is just sort of frozen for the time being while, you know, Ukraine is fighting for more of an advantage strategically. >> An important development that happened during the negotiations and surprised many people that the Trump administration showed its readiness to give Ukraine Article 5-like security guarantee and even even presented to Congress for approval. And Zelensky noted that they agreed the security bilateral security guarantee with the United States 100% of the text. And so basically, based on the reports and prior drafts, so under this guarantee there is an armed attack from Russia in the future, the United States will determine measures, including armed force in defense of Ukraine. So, Sam, you wrote an op-ed in December together with Jennifer Kavanagh from Defense Priorities arguing against such a security guarantee. Could you please lay out logic behind your arguments? >> Sure. I mean, the the basic problem with it is it's not credible. And that has to do with the fact that it it would depend on a Russian view that we would do something in the future that we're unwilling to do in the present, namely intervene directly to fight against Russia in Ukraine. And, you know, I think that inherently lacks credibility. And, you know, for the US, there are secondary problems here, which is um that if this guarantee were uh comes to fruition and were tested by Russia and Ukraine, it would call into question all of the US's other, you know, global security commitments. And, you know, that it could in fact embroil the US in a war with Russia on Ukrainian territory that might escalate to nuclear level. So, but the main problem is the credibility problem. And it's hard to get around that. And I think that that the, you know, just in the way that you mentioned the formulation was made, it seems to me the choice is between something narrow and credible and something broad like this Article 5 style guarantee and not credible. I think we can make credible commitments based on our demonstrated ability to willingness to do certain things to help Ukraine. We can codify that and with the right kind of sort of snapback mechanisms. And that, I think, you know, that could affect Russia's calculus. I think we're going going for something like an Article 5 style guarantee is unlikely to be credible and could in fact induce Russia to test it so as to undermine the other US security commitments. >> Eric, what are your thoughts on that? >> Yeah, I mean, unfortunately, I agree that in this case it was another massive blunder by Steve Whitkoff to put this on the table as if one could just snap one's fingers, copy the language, and everything would be hunky-dory. Article 5 is about so much more than the text of the Washington Treaty. You know, it's about decades of >> [snorts] >> political and military commitments that are highly credible and deter Russia by dint of the assumption that an attack on any part of NATO territory would produce an American military response. I mean, you can debate how much Trump has undermined that, but I think there's still enough doubt in the Russians' mind about you know, what would happen that still NATO is able to deter, you know, an attack because of all of the suite of activities which includes, you know, the deployment of US forces and the stationing of US forces in Europe. So, you would have this alternative Ukrainian scenario where there would be a promise of US intervention, but no forces there. So, that's one. You have contingency planning. You have joint defense planning. You have all these other things that wouldn't exist in this case. And so, I do worry that it is a you know, a check that would be written without the money in the account. And it would be very worrying if if Russia tried to call our bluff on that. That said, I agree that there are credible commitments that can be made, and I wish that the conversation uh had focused more on, you know, what what it would look like to ramp up support for Ukraine concretely in case of a violation and to put the resources behind it to make that credible. I think that's completely within the powers of the United States. I think the support is there in Congress to do something like that. I think the support is there with the Europeans. Uh but it's a shame that actually both the US and Europe in different ways have been focusing on things that are very, very hard to see as achievable. In the European case, it's this um coalition of the willing and the deployment of forces into Ukraine. I mean, I think that's a that's a real long shot as well. But then, rather than broaden the discussion, they've just focused on these kind of you know, shiny headlines, whereas you could really envision an alternative that would be you know, maybe less uh headline-grabbing, but really focused on building up Ukraine's long-term military capacity and integration with with NATO militaries. And so, I hope that the next phase of the discussions were to focus a little bit more on that. >> So, let me play a devil's advocate. So, the arguments in Sam's piece with Jennifer Gaona, it seems that the the second and third argument about the um damage to credibility of US commitments and potential pressure on the US and risk of war, they actually can be used to make an argument that this will be credible. So, it's important for the United States to have credibility of its commitments, including NATO. That's why when the promise is made, keeping it is part of the national interest. And second, when push will come to show, there will be a lot of pressure on the US to keep its word. So, can it be and Russians would probably understand that. And if there is like 20% chance of war with the United States, that can deter them. And additionally, with all the other mechanisms that you mentioned, we we can use them together with the Article 5-like guarantee. So, there would be double deterrence from other elements. What do you think about that? Why do you think that this is inherently not credible? >> Well, it's inherently not credible because it promises to do something that the US in the future that the US is currently unwilling to do. >> But there is no promise right now. So, the addition will >> You're suggesting the commitment in itself would so radically change the US conception of its national interest that it would behave in a 180° difference from the way it is behaving now. When we have lots of evidence that the US is willing to intervene even without prior promises. Many US interventions came without, you know, the commitment actually existing to begin with. So, I mean, I think basically what that requires is to dismiss the sort of structural US uh national interest limitations in the context of Ukraine that had been revealed in the in the context of a war. And either we can pretend like they don't exist or and thus potentially be engaged in a bluff with potentially catastrophic consequences, you know, I and either way, either the US is intervening and um is engaged in a war or it's not and its credibility globally is called into into >> Yeah, and that's that's exactly this consequence of the reduced credibility. >> I'm not saying that the the uh if it were to happen that it necessarily wouldn't have any impact on Russian decision-making, um but there are two additional problems to the one where we're sort of trying to talk ourselves into tying the US hands and sort of altering its conception of national interest by creating a commitment. One is the reality that wars like this don't really end cleanly and the chances of war breaking out again after a ceasefire are quite high. So, you know, we're we're sort of extending that commitment in the context when we're we we might well much more so than in the context of um say, you know, extending it to West Germany when the line uh the inner German border was established even if it wasn't formally agreed. Here, you know, we have no guarantee that there's going to be a a a durable ceasefire. And, you know, the second piece of of it is this is from a negotiation process standpoint. So, as you noted, Mykhailo, this document was conceived of exclusively in the US-Ukraine context. So, we had we had a separate negotiation where, you know, the Russians and the Europeans were not involved, where the US, you know, allegedly has put these kinds of promises on paper. So, which gets to another problem with the way the negotiations have been structured. They've been conducted in silos. So, you have a US-Ukraine agreement, you've got a Europe-Ukraine agreement, you've got a US-Russia agreement. There's some 20 points vaguely operating in the ether. And that poses two issues, which is like we have no idea if the Russians have signed off on this US-Ukraine security guarantee that apparently is 100% or 90% or 95% agreed, and whether in fact they would agree to stop the war if it were issued. And second, it denies you trade space, right? So, like unless you have all the issues on the table with all the parties in the room, you can't actually identify trades. >> Yeah, do you think that might be still there might be still be a chance to get Russian approval of this commitment considering their position in 2022 in Istanbul talks. And also that such a commitment would would confirm Ukraine would provide Ukraine a commitment outside NATO, which should be satisfy Russian and desire not to have Ukraine in NATO. >> I mean, like I don't think we can look to Istanbul and say therefore Russia should accept this. The fundamental difference was that that was a collective multilateral security guarantee to which Russia would have been a party. This is not that, right? I think the question, Mykhailo, that you're asking is whether a commitment in and of itself is enough to sort of change the underlying calculus on what American leaders perceive to be the national interest. And that to me is is a real long shot. I mean, even Ukraine's most ardent supporters in Congress have not made the argument that the United States should intervene militarily on Ukraine's behalf. They've made the argument that we should provide everything in our arsenal and enable long-range strike and all of that kind of stuff. But they've never made the argument that the United States military should intervene. So, to me it would require such a fundamental shift in the logic of what our leaders in both parties perceive to be the national interest that I think a commitment on paper uh wouldn't be enough to do that. So, that gets me back to, you know, what is achievable in terms of codifying the types of things that we've already done and may and pushing the envelope there to make it a lot clearer to the Russians what the consequences would be if they invaded uh again. I don't actually think that that commitment in and of itself needs to be negotiated with Russia um which is where I might differ a bit with Sam. I think that it can be done in US-Ukraine channels. If it comes out of US-Ukraine channels and is truly credible in terms of reflecting what the United States has done and is willing to do and is presented to the Russians then again, I think that it will in our way create facts on the ground that they then have to contend with. And they will have to realize that like yes, the United States is prepared to make a long-term commitment to Ukraine's military and combat power. The Russians will say, well, they've been doing it for 4 years. They seem to be ready to continue that course. We're going to have to deal with that scenario. And I think then they will negotiate from the understanding that that is going to be the new status quo. And rather than put that up for negotiation, too we would kind of set our position as yes at a minimum there is going to be this long-term Western-Ukrainian military military military technical relationship and that is like a bare minimum that Russia is not going to be able to touch. So, again, I think we have leverage in order to make our commitment clearer and more credible through Congress with our allies, but that hasn't been the discussion uh because I think Witkoff and Kushner tried to take the easy way out and just propose something that sounded good on paper, but didn't really have anything behind it. >> Eric, so you mentioned about the snapback clause in a potential ceasefire where if Russia violates a ceasefire, there would be an increase of support from Western partners to Ukraine similar to one the West provided to Ukraine during this war. So, my question is, do you think that the that Ukraine would still have certain reservations about this because in Sam mentioned that ceasefires they often violated. That Russia already during this war already showed that it can incur a tremendous cost. That other leaders would not would would finish the war a long time ago. So, and Ukraine would still feel vulnerable to that because Russia may just think, "Yeah, okay. There will be the same level of support, but we want to achieve our objectives again." So, do you think what do you think about that? And and maybe the combination of this snapback provision with the Article 5 like guarantee may reinforce each other. >> Let me give the example on the intelligence side. So, there is draft language which is in the intelligence authorization act for this year which came out of committee, Senate Committee, in a bipartisan vote. It was supported by Senator Cotton and Senator Bennet. And again, ended up voted out of committee overwhelmingly. And it outlined a sort of three phase schema for our intelligence support. So, number one, that the United States would continue supporting Ukraine uh with kind of actionable military intelligence, targeting information, so on and so forth, as it has been doing until such time as there was an agreed ceasefire. Then, in phase two, that the United States would transition into a longer-term capacity building relationship between the US intelligence community and the Ukrainian services uh in sort of a kind of peacetime or armistice time scenario. And that three, if there were a uh a renewed invasion, that the United States would go back to providing the actionable, real-time uh targeting information that it'd been providing before. To me, that is eminently reasonable, and it does codify a future commitment that I think is credible in this case. If you were to broaden that out and, you know, look at the security assistance side, what I would say is that snapping back to what we originally did in February 2022, I mean, that's beside the point, because that was a completely different scenario where, up until the moment of the invasion, the United States had only provided Javelins and Stingers. We've since provided F-16s and tanks and HIMARS and ATACMS and all of that. So, the goalposts have moved substantially, but you take, you know, where we are now, you envision out what a sort of peacetime uh capacity building relationship would be with the Ukrainian military to ensure that, you know, it's combat capable, sustainable, and so on. And then, whether it's through the creation and resourcing of stockpiles, which could be located in parts of Europe, other parts of the world, etc. You know, we have such things for Israel. You know, again, you can get allies to contribute and then you could resource defense production plans to actually fill those stockpiles and then there would be a key kind of, you know, break glass sort of infrastructure where Russia would see, okay, well, you know, the Ukrainian military is continuing to be trained, reformed, etc., armed in this peacetime. It's still a significant force that we're going to have to contend with, unlike the Ukrainian military that existed in February '22. And in addition, there are all these other weapons that are sort of waiting in the wings and the US has promised to ship these to Ukraine in a moment of emergency. I mean, to me, that kind of comes together when you stitch these different agreements together, give it a name, give it a political governance mechanism that, you know, looks something more like the North Atlantic Council or whatever it is that would be the governance mechanism for Ukraine, the United States, and the key allies that sign on to this. I mean, to me, that looks pretty credible. And it's not Article 5 per se, but it is a pledge to do something in the future, which would be highly punitive to the Russians and it's much more credible than any of these vague commitments. >> Sam, what are your thoughts on that? >> So, I think that I would try to conceive of snapback as like total snapback so that any benefits that Russia receives from the a potential agreement would be tied to ceasefire compliance to include any commitments to military and political military restraint. So, it loses those two, and presumably those would be also valuable for Russia. And then I would just say, generally speaking, we're not talking about silver bullets of deterrence that if Russia is determined to go to war, these are not going to like be insurmountable roadblocks. What we're talking about is creating a sort of infrastructure of deterrence that, you know, can affect in some substantive way Russia's potential calculus in the future. And, you know, first and foremost, that's going to be a very capable Ukrainian military as it's serving as a deterrent, but these can contribute more. We shouldn't look to any single thing as the sort of silver bullet for fundamentally altering Russia's future calculus. >> Thank you, Sam and Eric, for the Lawfare Podcast. >> Thank you, Bohdan. >> [music] >> The Lawfare Podcast is produced by the Lawfare Institute. You can also find all of our written work at lawfaremedia.org. The podcast is edited by Jen Patja with audio engineering by Noam Osband of Goat Rodeo.
